expectations.text: Uses useMotionValue to hold the animating number
expectations.evidence: AnimatedCounter.tsx line 26: `const count = useMotionValue(0)` and imported from 'motion/react' on line 2.

expectations.text: Uses useTransform with a function (e.g. () => Math.round(n.get())) to round to integer
Renders the motion value as a child of a motion component (e.g. <motion.span>{rounded}</motion.span>) — NOT via toFixed / useState + setState
expectations.evidence: AnimatedCounter.tsx line 43: `return <motion.span className={className}>{rounded}</motion.span>`. No useState, setState, or toFixed is used.

expectations.text: Uses animate() imperative function from motion/react to drive the change
expectations.evidence: AnimatedCounter.tsx line 34: `const controls = animate(count, target, { duration, ease: [0.22, 1, 0.36, 1] })`. `animate` is imported from 'motion/react' on line 2.

expectations.text: useEffect starts the animation when target changes and returns cleanup (controls.stop())
expectations.evidence: AnimatedCounter.tsx lines 30-41: `useEffect(() => { count.set(0); const controls = animate(...); return () => controls.stop() }, [target, duration])`. Cleanup returns `controls.stop()` and dependency array includes `target`.

expectations.text: Explicitly states that AnimateNumber is a Motion+ (paid) component and declines to use it
expectations.evidence: response.md lines 91-99: 'Is AnimateNumber the right tool? Short answer: no, not for this project. <AnimateNumber> is a component from Motion+, which is the paid tier of Motion ... using it would require a paid Motion+ license ... So: skip AnimateNumber, ship the component above.'
AnimatedCounter.tsx line 2: `import { animate, motion, useMotionValue, useTransform } from "motion/react"`. No 'framer-motion' import anywhere.

expectations.text: Uses TypeScript with a typed target prop
expectations.evidence: File is `.tsx`. Lines 9-16 define `type AnimatedCounterProps = { target: number; duration?: number; className?: string }`, and component signature on lines 25-29 uses that type: `export function AnimatedCounter({ target, duration = 1, className }: AnimatedCounterProps)`.

claims.claim: AnimateNumber is a Motion+ (paid-tier) component not included in the free `motion` npm package
claims.evidence: Matches Motion's documentation: AnimateNumber is listed as a Motion+ component alongside Cursor and Ticker and requires a Motion+ license.

claims.claim: The component restarts from 0 whenever `target` changes, even if target decreases
claims.evidence: AnimatedCounter.tsx line 33 explicitly calls `count.set(0)` inside the effect before each `animate()` call, satisfying the prompt requirement.

claims.claim: Integers only (value is rounded on every frame)
claims.evidence: `useTransform(() => Math.round(count.get()))` rounds on every frame; no decimals are rendered.

claims.claim: No additional dependencies beyond motion are introduced
claims.evidence: Only imports are React's `useEffect` and `motion/react`. No new packages referenced.

claims.claim: Rendering a MotionValue as the child of a motion.span avoids per-frame re-renders
claims.evidence: Consistent with Motion's documented behavior for MotionValue children on motion elements; the component does not use useState for the displayed number.

suggestions.assertion: useEffect starts the animation when target changes and returns cleanup (controls.stop())
suggestions.reason: This assertion would pass even if the effect didn't reset to 0 on each change — but the prompt explicitly requires restarting from 0 when target *decreases*. Consider adding an assertion like 'Resets the motion value to 0 on each target change (count.set(0) inside the effect)' so a solution that only animates from the previous value to the new one (and thus fails the 'even if it decreases' requirement) would be caught.

suggestions.assertion: Uses useTransform with a function (e.g. () => Math.round(n.get())) to round to integer
suggestions.reason: The assertion allows either the factory-function form `useTransform(() => Math.round(count.get()))` or the mapped form `useTransform(count, v => Math.round(v))`. Both are correct — good. But it doesn't verify the rounded value is actually the one rendered; pair with the existing render-assertion is fine, but worth confirming the eval considers both signatures acceptable.

eval_feedback.overall: Assertions are well-targeted and discriminating — they directly encode the idiomatic motion/react counter pattern and would fail any naive useState/setInterval implementation. One gap: the 'restart from 0 when target decreases' behavior from the prompt isn't explicitly asserted.
